original_hypothesis.title: Fee Manipulation via Broken Access Control Enables Sell Lockout and Fund Trapping
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: Due to broken onlyManager modifier, anyone can call setMaxFeePercent followed by setExternalFeePercent to set total fees ≥ 100% (1 ether). When totalFee >= price, getSellPriceAfterFee underflows (price - totalFee), and _transferFees computes negative sellValue. Sellers cannot sell tokens, permanently trapping their funds in the bonding curve.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This combines the access control vulnerability with fee economics to create an irreversible fund-trapping scenario. Even after the fees are fixed, users who bought at high prices during manipulation may suffer losses.

proof_of_signal.pattern: sellValue = price - protocolFee - subjectFee - referralFee - holderFee — underflows if total fees >= price

preconditions: maxFeePercent has no hard upper bound vs 1 ether

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: setMaxFeePercent(2 ether) then setExternalFeePercent(0.5 ether, 0.3 ether, 0.3 ether) → fees = 110%+ of price
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Set fees > 100%, all sells revert with underflow, then extort users or wait
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker buys at normal fees, sets fees to >100%, nobody can sell, then resets fees and sells at profit as only seller

economic_questions: Can the attacker also set protocolFeePercent to redirect buy fees to themselves while sells are blocked?
Is there a Solidity 0.8.7 checked arithmetic revert in _transferFees for the underflow?
validation_requirements: Set fees above 100% and confirm sell reverts
Verify no other bounds check prevents this configuration

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: All tokens become unsellable — total bonding curve TVL at risk
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Infinite if combined with fee destination redirection

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All token holders across all curves

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm sell path reverts when totalFee > price

validation_result.mechanism: broken access control + unbounded fee percent = sell underflow DoS
validation_result.impact_summary: Anyone can set fees ≥ 100% via broken onlyManager modifier, causing all sellCurvesToken calls to revert with arithmetic underflow, permanently trapping user funds in the bonding curve.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The attack combines two code defects: (1) Security.sol modifiers `onlyOwner` and `onlyManager` are no-ops that never revert, making all protected functions publicly callable, and (2) `setMaxFeePercent` has no hard upper bound preventing fees from exceeding 100% of the price. An attacker calls `setMaxFeePercent(2 ether)` (succeeds because onlyManager doesn't revert for non-managers), then calls `setExternalFeePercent(0.5 ether, 0.3 ether, 0.3 ether)` setting total fees to 110%+. When any user tries to sell via `sellCurvesToken`, the `_transferFees` function computes `sellValue = price - protocolFee - subjectFee - referralFee - holderFee`. Since total fees exceed price, this subtraction underflows under Solidity 0.8.7 checked arithmetic, causing an automatic revert. All sells are blocked protocol-wide.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The broken modifiers in Security.sol lines 8-14 are confirmed as no-ops (boolean expressions evaluated as statements without require). The fee-setting functions in Curves.sol have no hard cap on maxFeePercent vs 1 ether. The sellValue calculation in _transferFees performs unchecked subtraction that will underflow when total fees exceed price.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The modifier evaluates `managers[msg.sender] == true` as a boolean expression statement that is discarded. There is no `require()` or `if(...) revert`, so the modifier never reverts regardless of caller identity. This makes all onlyManager-protected functions publicly callable.

No hard upper bound prevents maxFeePercent from being set above 1 ether (100%). The only check ensures current fee sum ≤ new max. An attacker can set maxFeePercent to 2 ether (200%), enabling subsequent fee percent values that sum above 100%.
After maxFeePercent is raised above 1 ether, the attacker can set individual fee percents that sum above 100%. The only validation is against maxFeePercent, which was already corrupted.

When total fees (protocolFee + subjectFee + referralFee + holderFee) exceed price, the `sellValue = price - protocolFee - subjectFee - referralFee - holderFee` computation causes an arithmetic underflow revert under Solidity 0.8.7 checked arithmetic. This makes all sell transactions revert.

calling_sequence: 1. Attacker calls setMaxFeePercent(2000000000000000000) [2 ether]. onlyManager modifier is a no-op (Security.sol:12-15). Current fees sum is 0 or small, so check passes. maxFeePercent is set to 2 ether.

2. Attacker calls setExternalFeePercent(500000000000000000, 300000000000000000, 300000000000000000) [0.5e, 0.3e, 0.3e]. onlyManager is no-op. protocolFeePercent + 0.5e + 0.3e + 0.3e = 1.1e ≤ 2e maxFeePercent. Fee percents are set.

3. Any user calls sellCurvesToken(subject, amount). Price is computed via getPrice(). getFees(price) returns: protocolFee + subjectFee + referralFee + holderFee = price * (protocolFeePercent + 0.5e + 0.3e + 0.3e) / 1e = price * 1.1 (at minimum) > price.
4. In _transferFees, sellValue = price - protocolFee - subjectFee - referralFee - holderFee. Since sum of fees > price, this underflows and reverts.
5. Transaction reverts. All sells are blocked. User funds are trapped.
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The attack path starts at Security.sol where the onlyManager modifier (lines 12-15) fails to enforce access control due to missing `require`. This allows any address to call setMaxFeePercent (Curves.sol:113) to raise the fee cap above 100%, then setExternalFeePercent (Curves.sol:135) to set individual fees summing above 100%. When sellCurvesToken (Curves.sol:258) is called, it invokes _transferFees (Curves.sol:192) which computes sellValue via subtraction (approximately line 204). With Solidity 0.8.7 checked arithmetic, the underflow causes an automatic revert, blocking all sells.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts directly set maxFeePercent or fee percentages. The vulnerability exists regardless of deployment configuration since the broken modifier allows anyone to change these values post-deployment.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Attack is fully validated: (1) Security.sol onlyManager modifier is confirmed as a no-op — `managers[msg.sender] == true;` is a statement with no effect. (2) setMaxFeePercent has no upper bound check against 1 ether. (3) setExternalFeePercent only checks against maxFeePercent, not against 100%. (4) _transferFees sellValue computation will underflow when fees > price. (5) Solidity 0.8.7 uses checked arithmetic, so the underflow causes a revert. The attack requires exactly two transactions and minimal gas cost.
validation_result.impact_assessment: CRITICAL. An attacker can permanently block all sell operations protocol-wide with two transactions costing only gas. All token holders across all curves would be unable to sell their tokens, effectively trapping all ETH locked in the bonding curve. The attacker could also set protocolFeePercent via setProtocolFeePercent (also unprotected via broken onlyOwner) to redirect buy fees to themselves while sells are blocked, extracting value from any new buyers.
recommended_mitigations: Fix Security.sol modifiers to use require(): `modifier onlyManager() { require(managers[msg.sender], "Not manager"); _; }`
Add a hard upper bound in setMaxFeePercent: `require(maxFeePercent_ <= 0.5 ether, "Max fee too high");` to ensure fees can never exceed 50% (or appropriate threshold below 100%)
Add a check in _transferFees for sells: `require(price > protocolFee + subjectFee + referralFee + holderFee, "Fees exceed price");` as a safety net
Consider adding a timelock on fee parameter changes to give users time to exit before new fees take effect
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy Curves with factory and FeeSplitter. 2. Subject buys first token. 3. Another user buys tokens at normal fees. 4. Attacker (any address) calls setMaxFeePercent(2e18). 5. Attacker calls setExternalFeePercent(5e17, 3e17, 3e17). 6. User attempts sellCurvesToken — expect revert with arithmetic underflow. 7. Verify getSellPriceAfterFee also reverts for confirmation.
uncertainties: The exact line numbers in proof_of_signal (213-218) may not precisely match the sellValue computation depending on how lines are counted, but the pattern and function are correct
additional_testing_needed: Verify the attack also blocks sellExternalCurvesToken which calls sellCurvesToken internally
Test whether buyCurvesToken also breaks (it should still work since buy path uses buyValue not sellValue, but msg.value requirement increases dramatically)
Confirm the attacker cannot also use setProtocolFeePercent to redirect buy-side fees to themselves simultaneously
